keyPoints: CT scan is most accurate imaging for adults
Laparoscopic appendectomy is standard of care
Antibiotics alone may be considered in select uncomplicated cases
Alvarado score helps stratify probability

female-28-appendicitis.name: J. Martin
female-28-appendicitis.chiefComplaint: Pain in my lower right belly since yesterday

missedConsequences.Acute Appendicitis: Patient sent home with 'gastroenteritis.' Appendix perforates 18 hours later. Returns with peritonitis, sepsis, and requires open laparotomy instead of laparoscopic approach. ICU stay required.
missedConsequences.Ectopic Pregnancy: Pregnancy test not ordered because patient 'uses condoms.' Actually pregnant with ectopic. Ruptures at home, hemorrhagic shock, emergency surgery.
missedConsequences.Ovarian Torsion: Assumed to be appendicitis without pelvic imaging. Torsed ovary becomes necrotic, requires oophorectomy.

commonPitfalls: Not asking about pain MIGRATION - periumbilical to RLQ is the most specific historical feature for appendicitis
Forgetting the sequence rule: in appendicitis, PAIN comes first, then nausea/vomiting. If vomiting precedes pain, think gastroenteritis
Assuming patient can't be pregnant because she uses contraception - ALWAYS get a pregnancy test in reproductive-age women
Missing that the patient 'lies still' - this is a key observation distinguishing peritonitis (lies still) from colic (restless)
Relying on CT alone - clinical diagnosis of appendicitis can be made without CT in classic presentations

mcqs.question: The patient reports pain that started around the umbilicus and moved to the RLQ. What is the pathophysiological explanation?

options.text: The appendix physically moved from one location to another
options.explanation: The appendix doesn't move. The pain pattern reflects different types of nerve fibers being activated.

options.text: Visceral pain (poorly localized) progressed to parietal pain (well-localized) as inflammation spread
options.explanation: Initially, visceral afferents sense appendiceal distension → referred to periumbilical area. As inflammation extends to parietal peritoneum, somatic afferents → localized RLQ pain.

options.text: The patient was confused about where the pain actually was
options.explanation: This is a real and reliable phenomenon, not patient confusion.

options.text: Gas moved through the intestines causing the pain to migrate
options.explanation: This describes colicky pain, not the progressive localization seen in appendicitis.

mcqs.question: You note the patient is lying very still and grimaces with any movement. A patient with kidney stones would likely behave differently. How?

options.text: Kidney stone patients also lie still
options.explanation: This is characteristic of peritonitis, not colic.

options.text: Kidney stone patients are typically restless, unable to find a comfortable position
options.explanation: Colicky pain (kidney stones, biliary colic) causes restlessness - patients writhe and can't get comfortable. Peritonitis patients lie perfectly still because movement hurts.

options.text: Kidney stone patients have higher pain tolerance
options.explanation: This is not about pain tolerance but about different pain mechanisms.

options.text: There is no difference in patient behavior
options.explanation: This behavioral difference is a classic clinical pearl.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Appendicitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Classic presentation: periumbilical pain migrating to RLQ, fever, anorexia, nausea/vomiting, positive McBurney's and Rovsing's signs. Textbook appendicitis.

allDiagnoses.name: Gastroenteritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting. But pain pattern, fever, and localized tenderness argue against this.

allDiagnoses.name: Ovarian Cyst Rupture
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause acute RLQ pain in young women. But no mid-cycle timing, and pain pattern is more consistent with appendicitis.

allDiagnoses.name: Ectopic Pregnancy
allDiagnoses.rationale: ALWAYS consider in reproductive-age woman with lower abdominal pain. Must get pregnancy test even though LMP was 3 weeks ago and she uses condoms.

allDiagnoses.name: Ovarian Torsion
allDiagnoses.rationale: Surgical emergency. Usually sudden onset severe pain with nausea. No adnexal mass on exam here.

allDiagnoses.name: Perforated Viscus
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect more diffuse peritonitis, rigid abdomen, more toxic appearance.

allDiagnoses.name: Mesenteric Lymphadenitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: More common in children. Can mimic appendicitis but usually associated with viral illness.

allDiagnoses.name: Crohn's Disease
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can present with RLQ pain (terminal ileitis). No prior history, no diarrhea, no weight loss.

allDiagnoses.name: UTI/Pyelonephritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: No urinary symptoms. Pain location not typical for kidney.

female-28-appendicitis.oldcartsNarrative: "The pain started yesterday afternoon around my belly button - kind of a dull ache. Then overnight it moved to my right lower side and got much sharper and worse. Now it's constant and really bad, like an 8 out of 10. It hurts more when I move or cough so I'm trying to stay still. I feel nauseous and threw up once this morning. I don't want to eat anything. I felt feverish last night with chills. I haven't had a bowel movement today and was a little constipated yesterday. My last period was 3 weeks ago and was normal. I'm sexually active with my boyfriend and we use condoms every time."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: It started yesterday afternoon, probably around 3 or 4pm. At first it was just a vague ache around my belly button, but then overnight it moved to my right side and got much worse.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Timeline is critical - 12-24 hour history is classic for appendicitis. Longer duration increases perforation risk.

historyQuestions.text: Where did the pain start and has it moved?
historyQuestions.answer: It started around my belly button yesterday afternoon. Then overnight it moved down to my right lower side. That's where it's been ever since.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CLASSIC MIGRATION PATTERN: Periumbilical → RLQ is pathognomonic for appendicitis. Visceral pain from appendiceal inflammation starts periumbilical, then localizes as parietal peritoneum becomes involved.

historyQuestions.text: Describe the pain - is it sharp, dull, crampy?
historyQuestions.answer: At first it was dull and achy. Now it's sharp and constant. It doesn't come and go - it's there all the time.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Evolution from dull visceral pain to sharp somatic pain indicates progression of appendiceal inflammation to involve parietal peritoneum.

historyQuestions.text: Does anything make the pain better or worse?
historyQuestions.answer: Moving makes it way worse. Coughing hurts. Even the car ride here was terrible with every bump. Lying completely still is the only thing that helps a little.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pain with movement indicates peritoneal irritation. Patients with peritonitis prefer to lie still - this is a key observation.

historyQuestions.text: How is your appetite?
historyQuestions.answer: I have no appetite at all. The thought of food makes me feel sick.
historyQuestions.reasoning: ANOREXIA is part of the classic appendicitis triad (anorexia, nausea/vomiting, RLQ pain). Almost universal finding.

historyQuestions.text: Any nausea or vomiting?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I've felt nauseous since last night and I threw up once this morning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nausea/vomiting typically follows onset of pain in appendicitis. If vomiting precedes pain, think gastroenteritis.

historyQuestions.text: When was your last menstrual period?
historyQuestions.answer: About 3 weeks ago. It was normal - came on time, normal flow, lasted about 5 days like usual.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Must ask in all reproductive-age women. LMP 3 weeks ago makes pregnancy less likely but doesn't rule it out.

historyQuestions.text: Is there any chance you could be pregnant?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't think so. I'm sexually active but we use condoms every time. I've never missed a pill or had a condom break.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Still need pregnancy test despite reported consistent contraception use. Ectopic pregnancy is life-threatening.

historyQuestions.text: Any vaginal bleeding or discharge?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No vaginal bleeding makes ectopic less likely, but doesn't rule it out.

historyQuestions.text: Any history of pelvic infections, STIs, or ectopic pregnancy?
historyQuestions.answer: No, never had any of that. I've been pretty healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No PID or ectopic history reduces risk, but still need pregnancy test.

historyQuestions.text: Have you felt feverish?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I felt hot last night and had chills. I didn't check my temperature but I definitely felt feverish.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Low-grade fever common with appendicitis. High fever (>102°F) suggests perforation.

historyQuestions.text: Any diarrhea or constipation?
historyQuestions.answer: No diarrhea. I was a little constipated yesterday and haven't gone today.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Constipation common with appendicitis. Diarrhea would suggest gastroenteritis.

historyQuestions.text: Any pain with urination or urinary frequency?
historyQuestions.answer: No, urination has been normal.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No urinary symptoms argues against UTI. However, pelvic appendix can irritate bladder and cause urinary symptoms.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries before?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I've never had any surgery. This is my first time in a hospital.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No prior surgery means appendix is in place and adhesions less likely

historyQuestions.text: Any allergies to medications?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies to anything as far as I know.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for surgical planning and antibiotic selection

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

diet_recent_changes.answer: Nothing unusual. Ate dinner last night, woke up with this pain.
diet_recent_changes.relevance: Diet generally not a factor in appendicitis; helps establish timeline and rule out food-related causes.

social_recent_travel.answer: No travel. Been home and at work all week.
social_recent_travel.relevance: No travel makes parasitic causes or traveler's diarrhea less likely; supports typical appendicitis presentation.

occupation_current_job.answer: Graduate student. Mostly sitting in lab and at my desk. Started feeling this during class.
occupation_current_job.relevance: Occupation less relevant to appendicitis; establishes onset setting.

social_sick_contacts.answer: No one else sick. My roommate is fine.
social_sick_contacts.relevance: No sick contacts makes viral gastroenteritis less likely; supports surgical etiology.

social_stress_factors.answer: Midterms coming up. Stressed but nothing new.
social_stress_factors.relevance: Stress doesn't cause appendicitis but worth noting for overall patient context.

housing_food_safety.answer: I eat from the dining hall mostly. Nothing unusual or suspicious.
housing_food_safety.relevance: No foodborne illness concerns that would suggest gastroenteritis.

social_sexual_history.answer: Sexually active with one partner. We use condoms.
social_sexual_history.relevance: Important in young woman with RLQ pain - must consider ectopic pregnancy and PID in differential.

recreation_recent_activities.answer: Nothing unusual. Gym a few days ago. Normal routine.
recreation_recent_activities.relevance: No trauma or unusual activities to explain abdominal pain.

physicalExam.finding: BP 112/70, HR 102 (tachycardic), RR 18, Temp 101.2°F (fever), SpO2 98%.
physicalExam.reasoning: Low-grade fever and tachycardia support infectious/inflammatory process. High fever would suggest perforation.

physicalExam.finding: Young woman in mild distress, lying very still. Prefers not to move. Grimaces with position changes.
physicalExam.reasoning: Lying still and reluctance to move are classic for peritonitis. Patients with colic (kidney stone, biliary) move around restlessly.

physicalExam.finding: Abdomen flat, no distension. No scars. No visible masses.
physicalExam.reasoning: Flat abdomen without distension. Scars would raise concern for adhesive obstruction.

physicalExam.finding: Hypoactive bowel sounds.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hypoactive bowel sounds common with peritoneal inflammation. Absent bowel sounds would suggest ileus or obstruction.

physicalExam.finding: Maximal tenderness in RLQ. Voluntary guarding present. No rigidity. Minimal tenderness in other quadrants.
physicalExam.reasoning: Localized RLQ tenderness with guarding is classic for appendicitis.

physicalExam.finding: POSITIVE - Exquisite tenderness at McBurney's point (1/3 distance from ASIS to umbilicus).
physicalExam.reasoning: MCBURNEY'S POINT TENDERNESS is the most sensitive sign for appendicitis. This is where the base of the appendix typically lies.

physicalExam.finding: POSITIVE - Palpation of LLQ causes pain in RLQ.
physicalExam.reasoning: ROVSING'S SIGN is positive when LLQ palpation causes RLQ pain. Indicates peritoneal irritation in RLQ.

physicalExam.finding: POSITIVE - Pain on release of pressure in RLQ.
physicalExam.reasoning: Rebound tenderness indicates parietal peritoneal inflammation.

physicalExam.finding: POSITIVE - Pain with passive right hip extension.
physicalExam.reasoning: Psoas sign suggests retrocecal appendix irritating the psoas muscle.

physicalExam.finding: POSITIVE - Pain with internal rotation of flexed right hip.
physicalExam.reasoning: Obturator sign suggests pelvic appendix near the obturator internus muscle.

physicalExam.finding: No adnexal masses or tenderness. No cervical motion tenderness. No vaginal discharge or bleeding.
physicalExam.reasoning: Normal pelvic exam argues against gynecologic pathology. No CMT reduces ectopic likelihood.

labs.name: Urine pregnancy test
labs.reasoning: MANDATORY in ALL reproductive-age women with abdominal pain. Rules out ectopic pregnancy. Do this first!

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 14,200 (elevated with 85% neutrophils - left shift), Hgb 13.5, Plt 280
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis with left shift supports acute bacterial infection/inflammation. Classic for appendicitis.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Baseline for surgery. Assess hydration status and renal function.

labs.name: Urinalysis
labs.result: Normal. No WBCs, no bacteria, no blood.
labs.reasoning: Rules out UTI. Note: pelvic/retrocecal appendix can cause mild sterile pyuria from inflammation.

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Rules out pancreatitis in patient with abdominal pain and vomiting.

labs.name: Liver Function Tests
labs.result: Normal AST, ALT, Alk Phos, Bilirubin.
labs.reasoning: Rules out biliary pathology. RLQ pain can occasionally be from gallbladder disease.

labs.name: Type and Screen
labs.result: Type O positive. Antibody screen negative.
labs.reasoning: Pre-operative preparation in case transfusion needed during surgery.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: Pre-operative baseline. Not routinely needed in young healthy patient.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Would be elevated in bowel ischemia or severe sepsis. Normal here is reassuring.

labs.name: C-Reactive Protein
labs.reasoning: Nonspecific inflammatory marker. Elevated in appendicitis but doesn't change management.

labs.name: Procalcitonin
labs.reasoning: Not routinely needed. CRP and WBC are sufficient. May be elevated with perforation.

labs.name: Stool cultures/studies
labs.result: Not indicated - this is not gastroenteritis.
labs.reasoning: Clinical presentation is appendicitis, not infectious diarrhea. Would delay diagnosis.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with IV contrast
imaging.result: Enlarged appendix (9mm diameter, normal <6mm) with periappendiceal fat stranding and wall enhancement. No perforation. No abscess. No free air.
imaging.reasoning: CT is gold standard for appendicitis diagnosis (sensitivity 94%, specificity 95%). Confirms diagnosis and rules out complications.

imaging.name: Pelvic/RLQ Ultrasound
imaging.result: Non-compressible tubular structure in RLQ measuring 8mm (appendix). No free fluid. No adnexal masses.
imaging.reasoning: First-line in pregnancy and children (no radiation). Less sensitive than CT but can be diagnostic if appendix visualized.

imaging.name: Abdominal X-ray
imaging.result: No free air. No bowel obstruction. Non-specific bowel gas pattern.
imaging.reasoning: Rarely helpful for appendicitis. May show fecalith in 5% of cases. Better for obstruction or perforation.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Clear lungs. No free air under diaphragm.
imaging.reasoning: Can see free air under diaphragm if perforation, but CT is better. Reasonable pre-op baseline.

imaging.name: MRI Abdomen
imaging.result: Alternative to CT in pregnancy if ultrasound non-diagnostic.
imaging.reasoning: Reserved for pregnant patients when US non-diagnostic. Avoids radiation.

procedures.name: IV access
procedures.result: 18-gauge IV placed for fluids and medications.
procedures.reasoning: Required for IV fluids, antibiotics, and anesthesia.

procedures.name: Foley catheter
procedures.result: Placed in OR prior to surgery.
procedures.reasoning: Often placed in OR. Monitors urine output during surgery.

procedures.name: Nasogastric tube
procedures.result: Not needed - no obstruction or significant vomiting.
procedures.reasoning: Reserved for patients with obstruction or intractable vomiting.

treatments.name: NPO (nothing by mouth)
treatments.result: Patient made NPO in preparation for surgery.
treatments.reasoning: Patient must be NPO for anesthesia. Reduces aspiration risk.

treatments.name: IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: Lactated Ringer's at 125 mL/hr started.
treatments.reasoning: Resuscitation and maintenance fluids. Correct any dehydration from vomiting/NPO.

treatments.name: IV Antibiotics (preoperative)
treatments.result: Cefoxitin 2g IV given preoperatively (covers gram-negatives and anaerobes).
treatments.reasoning: Pre-operative antibiotics reduce surgical site infection. Single agent covering enteric organisms preferred.

treatments.name: IV Pain medication (morphine/fentanyl)
treatments.result: Morphine 4mg IV for pain control.
treatments.reasoning: Appropriate pain control. Old teaching to withhold pain meds is WRONG - pain control doesn't mask exam findings.

treatments.name: Antiemetics (ondansetron)
treatments.result: Ondansetron 4mg IV for nausea.
treatments.reasoning: Controls nausea and prepares patient for surgery.

treatments.name: Oral antibiotics and discharge
treatments.result: INAPPROPRIATE - requires surgery.
treatments.reasoning: Antibiotics alone are NOT adequate treatment for appendicitis. Non-operative management has high failure rate.

treatments.name: Laxatives/enemas
treatments.result: CONTRAINDICATED in suspected appendicitis.
treatments.reasoning: Could precipitate perforation. Never give laxatives to patient with possible appendicitis.

treatments.name: NSAIDs (ketorolac)
treatments.result: Use cautiously - may mask fever. Morphine preferred.
treatments.reasoning: Can use for pain but may mask fever. Opioids often preferred pre-operatively.

consults.name: General Surgery consult - URGENT
consults.result: Surgery consulted. Plan for laparoscopic appendectomy within 24 hours.
consults.reasoning: Appendicitis requires surgical intervention. Earlier surgery = lower perforation rate.

consults.name: OB/GYN consult
consults.result: Not needed - pregnancy test negative, pelvic exam normal.
consults.reasoning: Would consult if concern for ovarian torsion, ectopic, or TOA. Not indicated here.

consults.name: Interventional Radiology
consults.result: Not indicated - no abscess requiring drainage.
consults.reasoning: IR drainage for appendiceal abscess if present. Not needed for uncomplicated appendicitis.

disposition.name: Operative management - laparoscopic appendectomy
disposition.result: Taken to OR for laparoscopic appendectomy. Acutely inflamed appendix removed. No perforation. Discharged POD1.
disposition.reasoning: Appendectomy is definitive treatment. Laparoscopic preferred - less pain, faster recovery, shorter hospital stay.

disposition.name: Admit for observation and serial exams
disposition.result: May be appropriate if diagnosis uncertain. Re-image if symptoms persist/worsen.
disposition.reasoning: For equivocal cases. If classic presentation with positive CT, proceed to surgery.

disposition.name: Discharge home with oral antibiotics
disposition.reasoning: Non-operative management has 30-40% failure rate. Risk of perforation, abscess, sepsis, death.

disposition.name: Discharge home without imaging
disposition.result: DANGEROUS - could miss appendicitis or ectopic
disposition.reasoning: Young woman with RLQ pain needs pregnancy test and imaging. Cannot discharge without workup.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Appendicitis
keyFindings: Classic pain migration: periumbilical → RLQ (pathognomonic)
Anorexia, nausea, vomiting - the appendicitis triad
Low-grade fever (101.2°F) with leukocytosis (WBC 14,200)
Positive physical exam signs: McBurney's, Rovsing's, rebound, psoas, obturator
Patient lies still - characteristic of peritonitis (vs. restless with colic)
CT confirms: enlarged appendix with periappendiceal fat stranding
Negative pregnancy test rules out ectopic
teachingPoints: PAIN MIGRATION IS PATHOGNOMONIC: Periumbilical → RLQ migration is the single most useful historical feature for appendicitis.
ANOREXIA IS ALMOST UNIVERSAL: If the patient is hungry, think twice about appendicitis.
SEQUENCE MATTERS: In appendicitis, pain comes first, THEN nausea/vomiting. If vomiting precedes pain, think gastroenteritis.
LYING STILL VS. RESTLESS: Peritonitis patients lie still (movement hurts). Colicky pain patients are restless (can't get comfortable).
ALWAYS GET PREGNANCY TEST: Non-negotiable in reproductive-age women with abdominal pain, regardless of contraception use.
MCBURNEY'S POINT: Located at 1/3 the distance from ASIS to umbilicus. Most sensitive exam finding.
CT IS GOLD STANDARD: >95% sensitivity and specificity. Ultrasound is first-line in pregnant patients and children.

guidelineReferences.name: SAGES Guidelines for Laparoscopic Appendectomy 2010
guidelineReferences.citation: Society of American Gastrointestinal and Endoscopic Surgeons
keyPoints: Laparoscopic appendectomy preferred over open in most cases
Benefits: less pain, faster recovery, lower wound infection rate
Early appendectomy (within 24 hours) recommended
Conversion to open may be necessary for complicated cases
Single-dose preoperative antibiotics reduce surgical site infection

guidelineReferences.name: WSES Jerusalem Guidelines for Acute Appendicitis 2020
guidelineReferences.citation: Di Saverio S et al. World J Emerg Surg. 2020;15(1):27
keyPoints: CT is gold standard for diagnosis in adults (sensitivity >95%)
Ultrasound first-line in pregnant women and children
Alvarado/AIR scores help risk stratification
Antibiotics-first approach only in highly selected uncomplicated cases
Early surgery (<24h) reduces perforation risk

alvarado.name: Alvarado Score (MANTRELS)
alvarado.description: Clinical scoring system for suspected appendicitis

thisPatient.nextStep: Surgical consultation; CT may not be necessary with classic presentation
thisPatient.clinicalAction: Score ≥7 = high probability. This patient has classic appendicitis - proceed to surgery.

low.action: Appendicitis unlikely; consider discharge with return precautions

air.name: AIR Score (Appendicitis Inflammatory Response)
air.description: Alternative to Alvarado with better discriminatory power
air.note: Includes CRP which improves specificity. Score 0-4 = low risk, 5-8 = intermediate, 9-12 = high risk
air.source: Andersson M et al. World J Surg. 2008;32(8):1843-1849

closureScript.diagnosis: Based on your symptoms and our examination, you have appendicitis - your appendix is inflamed and infected. The way your pain started around your belly button and then moved to your lower right side is very typical of this condition.
closureScript.plan: You're going to need surgery to remove your appendix. The surgeon will likely do this laparoscopically - through small incisions - which means a quicker recovery. We're going to get you started on IV antibiotics right now and give you some pain medication. The surgeon is on their way to see you.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Before surgery, let us know right away if your pain suddenly gets much worse or spreads across your whole belly - that could mean the appendix has ruptured and we'd need to move faster.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know surgery sounds scary, but this is one of the most common operations surgeons perform. Most people go home the next day and are back to normal activities within a week or two. You're young and healthy, which puts you in a great position for a smooth recovery.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Otherwise healthy and active. This illness is unexpected and concerning.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Young adult with supportive family and friends. Active social life.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

female-22-torsion.name: A. Kim
female-22-torsion.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe pain in my lower right belly

missedConsequences.Ovarian Torsion: Assumed to be appendicitis. CT shows 'normal appendix' but ovary not evaluated. Patient sent home. Ovary becomes necrotic over 24 hours, requires oophorectomy at age 22, impacts future fertility.
missedConsequences.Ectopic Pregnancy: Pregnancy test not done because patient has IUD. Ruptured ectopic causes hemorrhagic shock.
missedConsequences.Acute Appendicitis: If appendicitis were present and missed, progression to perforation and sepsis.

commonPitfalls: Assuming RLQ pain in women is always appendicitis - must consider ovarian pathology
Not recognizing SUDDEN ONSET severe pain as characteristic of torsion (vs. gradual onset in appendicitis)
Forgetting that nausea/vomiting occurs in torsion due to ovarian nerve stimulation
Not doing pelvic ultrasound when appendix appears normal on CT
Missing that patient has known ovarian cyst - cysts increase torsion risk
Thinking low-grade fever rules out torsion - can have mild fever from tissue ischemia

mcqs.question: This patient's pain started SUDDENLY and severely while she was jogging. How does this differ from typical appendicitis presentation?

options.text: There is no difference - both present suddenly
options.explanation: Appendicitis typically has gradual onset with migration pattern.

options.text: Appendicitis pain typically starts gradually and migrates; sudden severe onset suggests torsion
options.explanation: Ovarian torsion causes SUDDEN, severe pain when the ovary twists on its pedicle, cutting off blood supply. Appendicitis classically starts with dull periumbilical pain that gradually migrates to RLQ over 12-24 hours.

options.text: Exercise always causes appendicitis to present suddenly
options.explanation: Exercise doesn't change appendicitis presentation but can trigger torsion.

options.text: Age determines whether onset is sudden or gradual
options.explanation: Age doesn't determine pain onset pattern.

mcqs.question: Ultrasound shows an enlarged right ovary with decreased Doppler flow. What does this finding indicate?

options.text: Normal finding - ovarian blood flow varies
options.explanation: Decreased flow in enlarged ovary is pathologic.

options.text: Ovarian torsion with compromised blood supply - surgical emergency
options.explanation: Enlarged ovary + decreased/absent Doppler flow = torsion until proven otherwise. This is a TIME-SENSITIVE surgical emergency - ovary can become necrotic in 24-48 hours.

options.text: Ovarian cyst - can be managed conservatively
options.explanation: While cysts are common, decreased blood flow changes management entirely.

options.text: Ectopic pregnancy until proven otherwise
options.explanation: Ectopic pregnancy is in the fallopian tube, not the ovary.

allDiagnoses.name: Ovarian Torsion
allDiagnoses.rationale: ⭐ THE ACTUAL DIAGNOSIS. Sudden severe onset during exercise, known ovarian cyst, RLQ pain with nausea/vomiting. Classic presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Appendicitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Always on differential for RLQ pain. But sudden onset and known cyst make torsion more likely.

allDiagnoses.name: Ruptured Ovarian Cyst
allDiagnoses.rationale: Known cyst could rupture causing acute pain. But rupture usually causes brief sharp pain then gradual improvement.

allDiagnoses.name: Ectopic Pregnancy
allDiagnoses.rationale: ALWAYS consider. Patient has IUD which reduces but doesn't eliminate pregnancy risk. Must test.

allDiagnoses.name: Tubo-ovarian Abscess
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect higher fever, more toxic appearance, history of PID.

allDiagnoses.name: Kidney Stone
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can cause severe flank/abdominal pain but would expect colicky pattern, hematuria.

allDiagnoses.name: Gastroenteritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Doesn't explain sudden onset severe pain or known ovarian cyst.

female-22-torsion.oldcartsNarrative: "I was jogging this morning and suddenly got this horrible pain in my right lower belly - like someone stabbed me. It came on all at once, not gradually. The pain is constant and severe, like a 9 out of 10. I've been nauseous and threw up twice. I can't find any position that makes it better. I've felt a little feverish. I have a history of an ovarian cyst on my right side - they found it on ultrasound about 6 months ago and said to just watch it. My last period was 2 weeks ago and was normal. I have an IUD for birth control. The pain is so bad I can barely walk."

historyQuestions.text: Exactly how did the pain start?
historyQuestions.answer: I was jogging and it just hit me suddenly - like a switch flipped. One second I was fine, the next I was doubled over. It wasn't gradual at all.
historyQuestions.reasoning: SUDDEN ONSET is classic for torsion (ovary twists on its pedicle). Appendicitis typically has gradual onset over 12-24 hours with migration pattern.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me more about the ovarian cyst they found.
historyQuestions.answer: They found a cyst on my right ovary about 6 months ago when I had some cramping. They said it was about 5 centimeters and probably a functional cyst. They did a follow-up ultrasound 3 months later and it was still there but hadn't grown.
historyQuestions.reasoning: OVARIAN CYST IS MAJOR RISK FACTOR for torsion - enlarges the ovary and increases likelihood of twisting. 5cm cyst significantly increases torsion risk.

historyQuestions.text: Were you doing anything specific when the pain started?
historyQuestions.answer: I was jogging, about a mile into my run. Just running normally, nothing unusual.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Physical activity can trigger torsion by causing the enlarged ovary to twist. Exercise/position change are common precipitants.

historyQuestions.text: Is the pain constant or does it come and go?
historyQuestions.answer: It's constant and severe. Hasn't let up since it started. It's not crampy or coming in waves.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Constant severe pain suggests ongoing ischemia from torsion. Intermittent pain might suggest intermittent torsion/detorsion.

historyQuestions.text: When was your last menstrual period?
historyQuestions.answer: About 2 weeks ago. It was normal - same as always.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Mid-cycle timing - could be mittelschmerz if minor, but severity suggests more serious pathology. Still must rule out pregnancy.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your IUD.
historyQuestions.answer: I've had a hormonal IUD for about 2 years. No problems with it. It's been great for contraception.
historyQuestions.reasoning: IUDs are highly effective but not 100%. If pregnancy occurs with IUD, higher proportion are ectopic. Must still test.

physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia from pain. Low-grade fever can occur with tissue ischemia. Not as febrile as typical appendicitis.

physicalExam.finding: 22-year-old female appearing uncomfortable, holding her right lower abdomen. Diaphoretic. Having difficulty lying still due to pain.
physicalExam.reasoning: Severe distress suggests serious pathology. Interestingly, torsion patients are often restless (unlike peritonitis patients who lie still).

physicalExam.finding: RLQ tender to palpation. Mild guarding but no true rigidity. No rebound tenderness. Negative McBurney's point. Negative Rovsing's sign. Bowel sounds present.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tenderness without peritoneal signs argues against appendicitis. NEGATIVE McBurney's and Rovsing's make appendicitis less likely.

physicalExam.finding: Cervix appears normal, IUD strings visible. Mild right adnexal tenderness with a palpable fullness/mass on right. Cervical motion tenderness minimal. No vaginal discharge.
physicalExam.reasoning: RIGHT ADNEXAL MASS/FULLNESS with tenderness is key finding. This is the enlarged, torsed ovary.

physicalExam.finding: Normal tone. No masses. Mild tenderness on right but less than on pelvic exam.
physicalExam.reasoning: Helps assess for appendicitis (which can cause rectal tenderness) and stool impaction.

labs.name: Urine Pregnancy Test
labs.reasoning: MANDATORY: Must rule out pregnancy despite IUD. If pregnant with IUD, ectopic risk is higher.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Mild leukocytosis occurs with tissue ischemia. Not as elevated as would expect with appendicitis.

labs.name: BMP
labs.reasoning: Pre-operative baseline. Assess hydration status.

labs.name: Urinalysis
labs.result: Clear, no WBC, no RBC, no bacteria.
labs.reasoning: Rules out UTI/pyelonephritis as cause of pelvic pain.

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Rules out pancreatitis if upper abdominal component to pain.

labs.name: Type and Screen
labs.result: Type A positive. Antibody screen negative.
labs.reasoning: Pre-operative preparation. Blood may be needed if oophorectomy required.

labs.name: Quantitative serum β-hCG
labs.reasoning: Confirms urine pregnancy test. Some order to be certain before surgery.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Would be elevated in tissue necrosis/ischemia. Normal suggests ovary may still be viable.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: Pre-operative baseline.

imaging.name: Pelvic Ultrasound with Doppler - URGENT
imaging.result: Right ovary enlarged (6.2 cm) with a complex cystic structure. DECREASED DOPPLER FLOW to right ovary compared to left. Left ovary normal. No free fluid. Uterus normal with IUD in place.
imaging.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC STUDY OF CHOICE. Enlarged ovary + decreased/absent Doppler flow = torsion. Do this FIRST, don't wait for CT.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast
imaging.result: Appendix normal. Right ovary enlarged (6cm) with surrounding fat stranding. 'Whirlpool sign' suggesting torsion. Left ovary normal.
imaging.reasoning: CT can show ovarian enlargement but DOES NOT assess blood flow. Use if ultrasound non-diagnostic or to rule out appendicitis.

imaging.name: MRI Pelvis
imaging.result: Not needed - ultrasound is sufficient and faster.
imaging.reasoning: MRI rarely needed. Would delay surgery. Ultrasound with Doppler is diagnostic.

imaging.name: Abdominal X-ray
imaging.result: Non-specific. Not helpful for ovarian torsion.
imaging.reasoning: X-ray does not visualize ovaries. Not useful for this diagnosis.

procedures.name: IV access
procedures.reasoning: For fluids, pain medication, and anesthesia.

treatments.name: IV pain medication (morphine/fentanyl)
treatments.result: 4mg morphine IV given. Pain improved from 9/10 to 6/10.
treatments.reasoning: Pain control is appropriate while arranging surgery. Severe pain requires IV opioids.

treatments.name: IV fluids
treatments.result: Normal saline bolus started. Maintenance fluids initiated.
treatments.reasoning: NPO for surgery, maintain hydration. May be dehydrated from vomiting.

treatments.name: Ondansetron for nausea
treatments.result: 4mg ondansetron IV given. Nausea improved.
treatments.reasoning: Symptomatic treatment for nausea/vomiting.

treatments.name: NPO status
treatments.result: Made NPO for surgery.
treatments.reasoning: Patient needs to be NPO for anesthesia/surgery.

treatments.name: Prophylactic antibiotics
treatments.result: Cefazolin 2g IV given pre-operatively.
treatments.reasoning: Standard surgical prophylaxis before laparoscopy.

treatments.name: Oral pain medication and observation
treatments.result: INAPPROPRIATE - patient needs surgery.
treatments.reasoning: Torsion is a surgical emergency. Oral medication and observation risks ovarian necrosis.

treatments.name: Hormonal treatment to shrink cyst
treatments.result: INAPPROPRIATE for acute torsion.
treatments.reasoning: Hormonal therapy is for chronic cyst management, not acute torsion. Patient needs immediate surgery.

treatments.name: NSAIDs (ketorolac)
treatments.result: Can add for pain but opioids are primary treatment for this severity.
treatments.reasoning: Adjunct for pain. Does not replace opioids in severe acute pain.

consults.name: OB/GYN Surgery - STAT
consults.result: GYN resident at bedside within 15 minutes. Patient consented for diagnostic laparoscopy with possible detorsion vs oophorectomy.
consults.reasoning: SURGICAL EMERGENCY. GYN surgery is required. Time-sensitive - ovarian viability decreases after 24-48 hours.

consults.name: General Surgery consult
consults.result: Not primary consult - this is GYN pathology.
consults.reasoning: Ovarian torsion is managed by GYN surgery, not general surgery.

disposition.name: Emergent surgery (laparoscopy)
disposition.result: Laparoscopy performed. Right ovary found to be torsed 720 degrees. Ovary still viable (dusky but pinking up after detorsion). Detorsion performed successfully. Ovarian cystectomy performed. Ovary preserved.
disposition.reasoning: Surgery confirmed torsion. Fortunately caught in time to save the ovary. Cyst removed to prevent recurrence.

disposition.name: Admit for observation
disposition.reasoning: Observation delays surgery and increases risk of ovarian necrosis. Must operate urgently.

disposition.name: Discharge with follow-up
disposition.result: DANGEROUS - patient will lose her ovary.
disposition.reasoning: Torsion is a surgical emergency. Discharge would result in ovarian necrosis and loss of fertility on that side.

actualDiagnosis.name: Ovarian Torsion (Right) with Preserved Ovary
keyFindings: ⚠️ DIFFERENT DIAGNOSIS than appendicitis variant - same chief complaint!
SUDDEN ONSET severe pain during exercise - classic for torsion
Known 5cm ovarian cyst - major risk factor for torsion
Negative appendicitis signs (McBurney's, Rovsing's negative)
Palpable adnexal mass on pelvic exam
Ultrasound: enlarged ovary with DECREASED DOPPLER FLOW - diagnostic
Caught in time - ovary viable after detorsion
teachingPoints: ⚠️ SAME CHIEF COMPLAINT, DIFFERENT DIAGNOSIS: RLQ pain in young women requires considering BOTH appendicitis AND gynecologic emergencies.
SUDDEN vs GRADUAL ONSET: Torsion = sudden, severe (twisting). Appendicitis = gradual, migrating. This historical feature is crucial!
OVARIAN CYSTS INCREASE TORSION RISK: Enlarged ovary is more likely to twist on its pedicle. Always ask about known cysts.
DOPPLER ULTRASOUND IS KEY: CT cannot assess blood flow. Pelvic ultrasound with Doppler is the diagnostic study of choice for torsion.
TIME IS OVARY: Torsion is a surgical emergency. Ovarian viability decreases significantly after 24-48 hours of ischemia.
DON'T FORGET PREGNANCY TEST: Even with IUD. If pregnant with IUD, ectopic risk is higher.
PERITONEAL SIGNS DIFFER: Appendicitis causes peritoneal inflammation (positive McBurney's, rebound). Torsion doesn't initially involve peritoneum.

guidelineReferences.name: ACOG Practice Bulletin: Adnexal Torsion 2019
guidelineReferences.citation: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists. Obstet Gynecol. 2019;134(2):e56-e63
keyPoints: Ovarian cyst >5cm increases torsion risk significantly
Ultrasound with Doppler is first-line imaging
Absence of Doppler flow is highly specific but not sensitive
Surgery should not be delayed for imaging if high clinical suspicion
Ovarian detorsion preferred over oophorectomy when viable

guidelineReferences.name: ESGE Recommendations on Management of Adnexal Torsion 2020
keyPoints: Laparoscopy is preferred surgical approach
Detorsion should be attempted regardless of ovarian appearance
Ovarian function often recovers even if initially dusky
Cystectomy after detorsion reduces recurrence risk
Time to surgery is critical - operate within 24 hours

closureScript.diagnosis: Ms. Kim, we've figured out what's causing your pain. Your right ovary has twisted on itself - we call this ovarian torsion. That cyst they found before made your ovary heavier and more likely to twist. When the ovary twists, it cuts off its own blood supply, which is why the pain is so severe.
closureScript.plan: You need surgery right away to untwist your ovary. The gynecologist is coming to talk to you now. They'll do this through small incisions using a camera - it's called laparoscopy. The goal is to untwist the ovary and save it. They'll also take care of that cyst so this doesn't happen again.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Time matters here - the longer the ovary stays twisted, the more damage it can sustain. That's why we're moving quickly.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know you're worried about your fertility. The good news is we caught this quickly. In most cases when we can untwist the ovary promptly, it recovers fully and continues to function normally. The surgeon will do everything possible to save your ovary.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Otherwise healthy and active. This illness is unexpected and concerning.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Young adult with supportive family and friends. Active social life.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the pregnancy test was positive?
whatIfScenarios.description: Urine hCG returns positive in this patient with RLQ pain

chiefComplaint.original: Pain in my lower right belly since yesterday
chiefComplaint.modified: Pain in my lower right belly since yesterday. I just found out I'm pregnant.

shouldDrop: Simple appendicitis (still possible but ectopic first)

feedback.correct: Positive hCG + pelvic pain = ECTOPIC until proven otherwise. This is a surgical emergency if ruptured. Need transvaginal ultrasound and serum hCG immediately.
feedback.partial: Good concern for ectopic. What's the next diagnostic step?
feedback.incorrect: Positive pregnancy + abdominal/pelvic pain = ECTOPIC PREGNANCY until proven otherwise. This can rupture and cause hemorrhagic shock. Immediate transvaginal ultrasound required.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Negative pregnancy test = ectopic ruled out, consider appendicitis, ovarian pathology. Positive pregnancy test = ectopic FIRST, then consider other causes. Can have appendicitis AND pregnancy (not ectopic) but ectopic is the emergency.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the abdomen was rigid?
whatIfScenarios.description: Patient has diffuse abdominal rigidity with rebound throughout

chiefComplaint.original: Pain in my lower right belly since yesterday
chiefComplaint.modified: Severe pain all over my belly. It started on the right but now it's everywhere and I can't move.

feedback.correct: Diffuse peritonitis = perforation has occurred. This is a surgical emergency. May need open laparotomy instead of laparoscopic approach. Broad-spectrum antibiotics immediately.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of peritonitis. What does diffuse involvement suggest?
feedback.incorrect: Board-like rigidity with diffuse rebound = PERITONITIS from perforation. This is no longer 'simple' appendicitis. Needs emergent surgery, broad-spectrum antibiotics covering gram-negatives and anaerobes.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Localized RLQ tenderness = early/uncomplicated appendicitis, laparoscopic appendectomy. Diffuse peritonitis = perforation has occurred, more complex surgery, higher morbidity, longer hospital stay.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient was male?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same RLQ pain presentation but in a 28-year-old male

chiefComplaint.original: Pain in my lower right belly since yesterday
chiefComplaint.modified: Pain in my lower right belly since yesterday. Also my right testicle feels weird.

feedback.correct: In males, don't forget the testicle! Torsion can refer pain to lower abdomen, and appendicitis can cause testicular symptoms. Always examine the testicle.
feedback.partial: Good consideration of male-specific pathology. What testicular emergency can present with abdominal pain?
feedback.incorrect: In males with RLQ pain, always examine the testicle. Testicular torsion can present as abdominal pain, and incarcerated inguinal hernia can mimic appendicitis. Also consider epididymitis.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Female RLQ pain = consider ovarian/tubal pathology. Male RLQ pain = consider testicular torsion, epididymitis, incarcerated hernia. Always examine the genitalia in both sexes.

guidelineReferences.name: AHS Acute Migraine Treatment Guidelines 2021
keyPoints: Triptans remain first-line for acute moderate-severe migraine
NSAIDs effective for mild-moderate attacks
Red flags (SNOOP): Systemic symptoms, Neurologic signs, Onset sudden, Older age, Pattern change
CGRP antagonists (gepants) alternative for triptan failures
Avoid opioids - cause medication overuse headache
CT/LP if thunderclap headache to rule out SAH

female-32-migraine.name: A. Chen
female-32-migraine.chiefComplaint: Terrible headache with nausea and light sensitivity
female-32-migraine.variantDescription: Classic migraine with aura - benign diagnosis

missedConsequences.Migraine: If correctly diagnosed, appropriate treatment improves quality of life. No adverse outcome from diagnosis.
missedConsequences.Subarachnoid Hemorrhage: If SAH were present and missed, patient could rebleed and die. Must know red flags that aren't present here.
missedConsequences.Meningitis: If meningitis were present and missed, could be fatal. No fever or neck stiffness here.

commonPitfalls: Ordering CT scan for every headache - unnecessary radiation and cost for typical migraine
Not asking about aura symptoms - visual changes preceding headache are diagnostic for migraine with aura
Missing the triggers - stress, sleep changes, menstrual cycle are common migraine triggers
Forgetting to ask about similar prior headaches - migraine is typically recurrent
Not asking about family history - migraine has strong genetic component

mcqs.question: The patient describes seeing zigzag lines for 20 minutes before her headache started. What is this called and what does it indicate?

options.text: Photophobia - indicates need for CT scan
options.explanation: Photophobia is light sensitivity during the headache, not visual phenomena before.

options.text: Visual aura - strongly supports migraine diagnosis
options.explanation: Visual aura (scintillating scotoma, zigzag lines, fortification spectra) preceding headache by 15-60 minutes is classic for MIGRAINE WITH AURA. It results from cortical spreading depression.

options.text: Retinal detachment - ophthalmology emergency
options.explanation: Retinal detachment causes persistent visual loss, not temporary phenomena before headache.

options.text: Transient ischemic attack
options.explanation: TIA can cause visual symptoms but they don't typically precede headache in this pattern.

mcqs.question: This patient has a normal neurological exam and typical migraine features. What imaging is indicated?

options.text: CT Head - to rule out bleed
options.explanation: CT is not indicated for typical migraine presentation without red flags.

options.text: MRI Brain - to look for structural lesions
options.explanation: MRI not routinely indicated for typical migraine.

options.text: No imaging needed - this is a clinical diagnosis
options.explanation: Migraine is a CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS. Imaging is only indicated for red flags: worst headache of life, thunderclap onset, focal neurological deficits, papilledema, fever, or atypical features.

options.text: CT Angiogram - to evaluate vessels
options.explanation: CTA not indicated for typical migraine.

allDiagnoses.name: Migraine with Aura
allDiagnoses.rationale: ⭐ ACTUAL DIAGNOSIS. Visual aura, unilateral throbbing headache, photophobia, nausea, similar prior episodes, family history.

allDiagnoses.name: Tension-Type Headache
allDiagnoses.rationale: Most common headache type, but bilateral, pressing quality, no aura, no nausea - doesn't fit this presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
allDiagnoses.rationale: Always consider with severe headache. Red flags ABSENT here: not thunderclap, not worst-ever, no neck stiffness, no focal deficits.

allDiagnoses.name: Meningitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect fever, neck stiffness, toxic appearance. Absent here.

allDiagnoses.name: Intracranial Mass
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect focal deficits, papilledema, progressive pattern. Absent here.

allDiagnoses.name: Cluster Headache
allDiagnoses.rationale: Unilateral but periorbital, with autonomic features (tearing, rhinorrhea). Different pattern.

allDiagnoses.name: Medication Overuse Headache
allDiagnoses.rationale: Ask about analgesic use. Can complicate migraine.

female-32-migraine.oldcartsNarrative: "This headache started about 4 hours ago. Before the headache, I saw these weird zigzag shimmering lines in my vision for about 20 minutes - then they went away and the headache started. The pain is on the right side of my head, throbbing and pounding. It's about an 8 out of 10. Light makes it worse - I've been lying in a dark room. I've been nauseous and threw up once. I've had headaches like this before, maybe 2-3 times a year since my 20s. They're always the same - the vision thing, then the headache, then I feel wiped out afterward. My mom gets the same thing. I've been stressed at work and haven't been sleeping well. I'm on birth control pills but nothing else."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the visual symptoms before the headache.
historyQuestions.answer: I saw these zigzag shimmery lines, almost like looking through cracked glass. They started in the center of my vision and spread out over about 20 minutes, then faded away. That's when the headache started.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CLASSIC VISUAL AURA. Scintillating scotoma with fortification spectra, spreading over 20 minutes. Pathognomonic for migraine with aura.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had headaches like this before?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I've gotten these 2-3 times a year since my early 20s. They're always the same pattern - the vision thing, then the headache, then I feel washed out for a day after.
historyQuestions.reasoning: RECURRENT STEREOTYPED EPISODES - key migraine feature. Same pattern each time over years supports benign diagnosis.

historyQuestions.text: Does anyone in your family get headaches?
historyQuestions.answer: My mom has always gotten migraines, and so did my grandmother.
historyQuestions.reasoning: POSITIVE FAMILY HISTORY - migraine has strong genetic component. First-degree relatives have 50% increased risk.

historyQuestions.text: How did the headache start?
historyQuestions.answer: It built up gradually after my vision symptoms went away. Over about 30 minutes it went from mild to severe.
historyQuestions.reasoning: GRADUAL ONSET rules out thunderclap headache. SAH classically presents with sudden, maximal-at-onset 'worst headache of life.'

historyQuestions.text: Is this the worst headache you've ever had?
historyQuestions.answer: It's bad, but it's similar to my other migraines. I wouldn't say it's the worst ever.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 'Worst headache of life' is red flag for SAH. This headache is typical for patient = reassuring.

historyQuestions.text: What's been going on in your life recently?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been really stressed at work, not sleeping well, maybe getting 5-6 hours. I'm also about to get my period.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Classic migraine triggers: stress, sleep deprivation, menstrual cycle (estrogen withdrawal).

historyQuestions.text: Where exactly is the pain and what does it feel like?
historyQuestions.answer: It's on the right side of my head, behind my eye and into my temple. It's throbbing and pounding - I can feel my heartbeat in it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Unilateral, throbbing quality is classic migraine feature. Bilateral pressing would suggest tension-type.

historyQuestions.text: Any nausea, vomiting, or sensitivity to light or sound?
historyQuestions.answer: Very nauseous - I threw up once. Light makes it so much worse, and sounds are really irritating too. I just want to be in a quiet dark room.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Nausea/vomiting, photophobia, phonophobia are key migraine associated symptoms

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you on?
historyQuestions.answer: Just birth control pills. I take ibuprofen when I get these headaches but haven't taken any yet today.
historyQuestions.reasoning: OCPs can affect migraine patterns; migraine with aura + OCPs = increased stroke risk

historyQuestions.text: Any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Important before prescribing migraine treatment

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I'm healthy. Just these migraines that I've had for years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms otherwise healthy patient with chronic migraine

historyQuestions.text: Any stiffness in your neck?
historyQuestions.answer: No, my neck feels fine. I don't have any trouble moving it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Absence of neck stiffness argues against meningitis and SAH - important negative finding

historyQuestions.text: Any fevers or chills?
historyQuestions.answer: No fever. I feel cold if anything because of the nausea.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Afebrile - argues strongly against meningitis

historyQuestions.text: Any weakness, numbness, trouble speaking, or vision changes other than the zigzag lines?
historyQuestions.answer: No, just the visual aura before the headache and then the headache itself. My vision is normal now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: No focal neurological symptoms beyond typical migraine aura - reassuring

occupation_current_work_stress.answer: I'm a lawyer. Constant deadlines, high pressure. This case I'm working on is particularly stressful.
occupation_current_work_stress.relevance: Occupational stress is major migraine trigger. Identifying work factors helps with prevention strategies.

occupation_screen_time.answer: I stare at a computer all day. Sometimes 10-12 hours. My eyes get tired.
occupation_screen_time.relevance: Prolonged screen time contributes to eye strain and can trigger migraines. Blue light exposure affects sleep.

social_sleep_patterns.answer: Maybe 5-6 hours on work nights. I stay up late working. Sleep in on weekends.
social_sleep_patterns.relevance: Sleep deprivation and irregular sleep schedule are major migraine triggers. Key modifiable factor.

diet_caffeine_intake.answer: I drink a lot of coffee. Maybe 4-5 cups during the day to stay alert.
diet_caffeine_intake.relevance: High caffeine intake can cause rebound headaches; caffeine withdrawal on weekends may trigger migraines.

diet_meal_patterns.answer: I skip meals when I'm busy. Sometimes I don't eat until dinner.
diet_meal_patterns.relevance: Skipped meals and fasting are common migraine triggers due to hypoglycemia.

housing_lighting.answer: Fluorescent lights at the office. They flicker sometimes. Really bright.
housing_lighting.relevance: Fluorescent lighting and flicker can trigger photosensitive migraines.

diet_food_triggers.answer: I've noticed red wine sometimes brings one on. Aged cheese too, maybe.
diet_food_triggers.relevance: Patient identifies personal triggers (tyramine in wine/cheese) - important for prevention counseling.

social_hydration.answer: I probably don't drink enough water. Mostly coffee and tea.
social_hydration.relevance: Dehydration is common migraine trigger; caffeine is a diuretic that may worsen this.

physicalExam.finding: BP 118/76, HR 78, RR 14, Temp 98.4°F, SpO2 99%. All normal, afebrile.
physicalExam.reasoning: Afebrile argues against meningitis. Normal BP argues against hypertensive emergency.

physicalExam.finding: 32-year-old woman lying in darkened room, eyes closed, appears uncomfortable but not toxic. Covering eyes when lights on.
physicalExam.reasoning: Photophobia (light aversion) is classic migraine feature. Non-toxic appearance argues against meningitis.

physicalExam.finding: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Pupils equal, reactive. Visual fields full. Motor 5/5 throughout. Sensory intact. Reflexes 2+ symmetric. Coordination normal. Gait normal.
physicalExam.reasoning: NORMAL NEUROLOGICAL EXAM is reassuring. Focal deficits would warrant imaging.

physicalExam.finding: Sharp disc margins bilaterally. No papilledema. Normal venous pulsations.
physicalExam.reasoning: NO PAPILLEDEMA rules out elevated intracranial pressure from mass or venous thrombosis.

physicalExam.finding: Neck supple. No meningismus. Negative Kernig's and Brudzinski's signs.
physicalExam.reasoning: NO MENINGISMUS rules against SAH and meningitis. Key exam for any severe headache.

labs.name: No labs indicated for typical migraine
labs.result: Classic migraine presentation with normal neuro exam - labs are NOT needed.
labs.reasoning: Migraine is a CLINICAL diagnosis. Ordering labs wastes resources and delays treatment. Labs only indicated if atypical features or concern for secondary cause.

labs.name: Urine Pregnancy Test
labs.result: Negative. Important before prescribing triptans or ergots.
labs.reasoning: MUST check in reproductive-age women. Triptans category C, ergots contraindicated in pregnancy. Also affects OCP counseling.

labs.name: CBC/CMP (if atypical features)
labs.result: NOT indicated for this typical presentation.
labs.reasoning: Would only order if concern for infection, metabolic cause, or systemic illness. Not needed here.

imaging.name: No imaging indicated
imaging.result: Typical migraine with aura, normal neurological exam - imaging NOT needed.
imaging.reasoning: ACR Appropriateness Criteria: imaging NOT indicated for typical migraine without red flags. Red flags (SNOOP4): Systemic symptoms, Neurologic signs, Onset sudden, Older age >50, Pattern change, Positional, Precipitated by Valsalva, Papilledema.

imaging.name: CT Head (AVOID in typical migraine)
imaging.result: OVERUTILIZATION - not indicated for typical migraine with normal exam.
imaging.reasoning: Unnecessary radiation, cost, and false reassurance. CT would only be indicated for thunderclap headache, trauma, or focal deficits.

imaging.name: MRI Brain (AVOID in typical migraine)
imaging.reasoning: MRI can show incidental white matter lesions in migraine patients that cause anxiety. Only indicated for atypical features.

procedures.name: IV Access
procedures.reasoning: IV access needed for anti-emetics, fluids, and IV abortive medications.

procedures.name: Dark, Quiet Environment
procedures.result: Patient placed in dimmed room with minimal stimulation.
procedures.reasoning: Photophobia and phonophobia are cardinal migraine features. Environmental modification is therapeutic.

treatments.name: Sumatriptan 100mg PO (or 6mg SQ)
treatments.result: Sumatriptan 100mg PO given. Significant headache relief within 1 hour. Could also use SQ if vomiting.
treatments.reasoning: Triptans are FIRST-LINE abortive therapy for moderate-severe migraine. 5-HT1B/1D receptor agonists cause vasoconstriction and inhibit trigeminal activation. Most effective if given early.

treatments.name: Metoclopramide 10mg IV
treatments.result: Metoclopramide 10mg IV given. Nausea resolved, also helps headache.
treatments.reasoning: Metoclopramide is preferred antiemetic - also has efficacy for headache itself (dopamine antagonist). Give with diphenhydramine to prevent akathisia.

treatments.name: Ketorolac 30mg IV (or 15mg if <50kg)
treatments.result: Ketorolac 30mg IV given. Additional analgesia.
treatments.reasoning: NSAIDs are effective adjunct for migraine. Ketorolac useful when oral not tolerated. Can use alone for mild-moderate or with triptan for severe.

treatments.name: Diphenhydramine 25mg IV
treatments.result: Diphenhydramine 25mg IV given with metoclopramide to prevent akathisia.
treatments.reasoning: Prevents restlessness/akathisia from dopamine antagonists. Also has mild analgesic and sedative properties.

treatments.name: IV Normal Saline 1L
treatments.result: 1L NS given for rehydration.
treatments.reasoning: Treats dehydration from vomiting and poor intake. Dehydration itself is a migraine trigger.

treatments.name: Dexamethasone 10mg IV (for recurrence prevention)
treatments.result: Dexamethasone 10mg IV given to reduce migraine recurrence.
treatments.reasoning: Single dose reduces migraine recurrence over next 24-72 hours (NNT ~9). Consider for patients with history of recurrent ER visits.

treatments.name: Magnesium Sulfate 2g IV (if aura or refractory)
treatments.result: Magnesium 2g IV given over 20 minutes.
treatments.reasoning: Evidence supports magnesium for migraine with aura. Low risk, may provide additional benefit. Also useful for pregnant patients when triptans contraindicated.

treatments.name: OCP Counseling (CRITICAL in migraine with aura)
treatments.result: Discussed increased stroke risk with estrogen-containing OCPs in migraine WITH AURA. Recommend progestin-only or non-hormonal contraception.
treatments.reasoning: Migraine WITH AURA + estrogen-containing OCP = 2-4x increased stroke risk (already elevated in migraine with aura). Must counsel and consider alternative contraception.

consults.name: Neurology Referral (outpatient)
consults.result: Referral placed for migraine management and prevention.
consults.reasoning: Consider neurology referral for: frequent migraines (>4/month), refractory migraines, need for preventive therapy, atypical features.

consults.name: OB/GYN for contraception counseling
consults.result: Referral for alternative contraception options given migraine with aura.
consults.reasoning: Important to coordinate contraception change. Progestin-only pills, IUD, or barrier methods are safer options.

disposition.name: Discharge with Migraine Action Plan
disposition.result: Discharged after headache resolved. Given triptan prescription, prevention counseling, and PCP follow-up.
disposition.reasoning: Appropriate discharge once acute migraine treated and patient improved.

disposition.name: Discharge Prescriptions
disposition.result: Sumatriptan 100mg #9 (limit 2 days/week to avoid MOH). Ondansetron 4mg #6 for nausea. Naproxen 500mg for mild attacks.
disposition.reasoning: Triptan for moderate-severe attacks, NSAID for mild attacks. Limit triptan use to prevent medication overuse headache (MOH).

disposition.name: Trigger Avoidance Counseling
disposition.result: Counseled on: sleep hygiene (regular schedule), hydration, regular meals, caffeine moderation, stress management. Given headache diary.
disposition.reasoning: Lifestyle modification is first-line prevention. Headache diary helps identify triggers. Key modifiable factors: sleep, stress, caffeine, meals, hydration.

disposition.name: Discuss Preventive Medications (if frequent)
disposition.result: Discussed starting preventive therapy if migraines >4/month: propranolol, topiramate, or amitriptyline options.
disposition.reasoning: Preventive therapy indicated if: ≥4 headache days/month, significant disability, contraindication to abortives, or medication overuse. PCP or neuro to initiate.

disposition.name: Return Precautions
disposition.result: Return for: worst headache of life, fever, stiff neck, confusion, weakness, vision loss, or headache different from usual migraines.
disposition.reasoning: Patient must know red flags. New/different headache pattern warrants evaluation for secondary cause.

actualDiagnosis.name: Migraine with Aura
keyFindings: Visual aura (scintillating scotoma) preceding headache by 20 minutes
Unilateral, throbbing headache
Associated photophobia and nausea/vomiting
Recurrent stereotyped episodes over years
Strong family history (mother, grandmother)
Known triggers present (stress, sleep deprivation, menstrual)
NORMAL neurological exam, no meningismus, no papilledema
teachingPoints: MIGRAINE IS A CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS: No test 'proves' migraine. Diagnosis is based on history and exclusion of red flags.
AURA IS DIAGNOSTIC: Visual aura (scintillating scotoma, fortification spectra) preceding headache strongly supports migraine.
RED FLAGS FOR SECONDARY HEADACHE: Thunderclap onset, worst-ever, fever, neck stiffness, focal neuro deficits, papilledema, altered mental status, age >50 new headache.
DON'T SCAN EVERY HEADACHE: Imaging only indicated for red flags. CT exposes patient to radiation, increases cost, and can find incidental findings that cause anxiety.
TRIPTANS ARE FIRST-LINE: 5-HT1B/1D agonists abort migraine attacks. Work best when taken early. Contraindicated in CAD/uncontrolled HTN.
CONSIDER PREVENTION: If frequent migraines (>4/month), discuss prophylaxis: beta-blockers, topiramate, amitriptyline, CGRP inhibitors.
RECURRENCE IS EXPECTED: Migraine is a chronic condition. Education about triggers, lifestyle, and when to seek care is important.

guidelineReferences.name: AHS Consensus Statement: Acute Treatment of Migraine in Adults
keyPoints: Triptans are first-line for moderate-severe migraine attacks
NSAIDs effective for mild-moderate attacks
Early treatment (within 1 hour of onset) improves efficacy
Combination therapy (triptan + NSAID) may be more effective than either alone
Limit acute medication use to prevent medication overuse headache

guidelineReferences.name: AAN/AHS Guideline: Prevention of Migraine in Adults
keyPoints: Consider prevention if ≥4 headache days/month or significant disability
Level A evidence: divalproex, topiramate, metoprolol, propranolol, timolol
Level B evidence: amitriptyline, venlafaxine, atenolol, nadolol
CGRP monoclonal antibodies (newer): effective for episodic and chronic migraine
Migraine with aura: avoid estrogen-containing contraceptives (increased stroke risk)

closureScript.diagnosis: This is a migraine headache. The vision changes you had before the pain - those zigzag lines - are what we call an aura, and they're very typical for this type of migraine. The fact that you've had similar episodes before, your mom gets them too, and you've been stressed and not sleeping well all fit the pattern.
closureScript.plan: I'm giving you a medication called sumatriptan that works specifically for migraines. You're already starting to feel better. I'm also giving you a prescription to take home for future migraines - take it as soon as you feel one starting. Since you're having these a few times a year, you might want to talk to your doctor about preventive medication if they start happening more often.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Come back if you have a headache that's completely different from your usual migraines, a sudden severe 'thunderclap' headache that's the worst of your life, headache with fever and stiff neck, or any new weakness, numbness, vision loss, or confusion.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know migraines are miserable, and it can be scary when the pain is this bad. But your exam is completely normal, this fits your typical pattern, and we don't need any scans. The good news is migraines are very treatable.
