female-45-sah.name: D. Williams
female-45-sah.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe headache - worst of my life
female-45-sah.variantDescription: ⚠️ TRAP CASE: Thunderclap headache - Subarachnoid Hemorrhage

missedConsequences.Subarachnoid Hemorrhage: Patient told 'probably just a bad migraine' and discharged. Rebleeds 3 days later and dies. SAH rebleed has 50% mortality.
missedConsequences.Migraine: If this were migraine (it's not), treatment would be symptomatic only.

commonPitfalls: ⚠️ THIS IS A TRAP CASE - the patient says 'I get migraines' but this is NOT her typical migraine
Accepting 'migraine history' without exploring how THIS headache differs
Not recognizing 'worst headache of life' as a red flag requiring workup
Stopping workup after negative CT - LP is REQUIRED if CT negative but suspicion high
Attributing meningismus to 'muscle tension'
Missing that hypertension can be secondary to SAH (Cushing response)

mcqs.question: The patient says 'I get migraines' but describes this headache as different - sudden onset, maximal immediately, worst ever. What is the critical next question?

options.text: What medications do you usually take for migraines?
options.explanation: While relevant for treatment, this misses the urgent red flag.

options.text: How does this headache compare to your typical migraines?
options.explanation: CRITICAL QUESTION. 'Worst headache of life' that differs from usual pattern requires SAH workup regardless of migraine history. This patient's answer reveals this is NOT her typical migraine.

options.text: Have you been stressed lately?
options.explanation: Stress as migraine trigger is irrelevant given red flag presentation.

options.text: Do you have light sensitivity?
options.explanation: While photophobia can occur in both, it doesn't distinguish SAH from migraine.

mcqs.question: CT Head is negative for hemorrhage. Patient has nuchal rigidity. What is the next step?

options.text: Reassure and discharge - CT ruled out bleeding
options.explanation: DANGEROUS! CT sensitivity for SAH decreases over time. Negative CT does NOT rule out SAH.

options.text: Lumbar puncture to evaluate for xanthochromia
options.explanation: LP is REQUIRED after negative CT if SAH suspicion is high. CSF will show elevated RBCs and xanthochromia (yellow color from RBC breakdown). CT misses ~5% of SAH, more if >6 hours from onset.

options.text: Treat as migraine and observe
options.explanation: Meningismus + worst headache of life = SAH until proven otherwise.

options.text: MRI Brain to better evaluate
options.explanation: LP is the next step after negative CT. MRI can help but LP is diagnostic.

allDiagnoses.name: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
allDiagnoses.rationale: ⭐ ACTUAL DIAGNOSIS. Thunderclap headache, worst of life, differs from usual migraines, neck stiffness, elevated BP.

allDiagnoses.name: Migraine
allDiagnoses.rationale: Patient has migraine history, BUT this headache is different. Must not assume it's 'just another migraine.'

allDiagnoses.name: Ruptured Aneurysm
allDiagnoses.rationale: Most common cause of non-traumatic SAH. This patient likely has a ruptured berry aneurysm.

allDiagnoses.name: Meningitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can present with severe headache and neck stiffness. LP will differentiate.

allDiagnoses.name: Intracerebral Hemorrhage
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would show on CT. Different from SAH.

allDiagnoses.name: Tension Headache
allDiagnoses.rationale: Does not present with thunderclap onset or meningismus.

allDiagnoses.name: Hypertensive Emergency
allDiagnoses.rationale: Elevated BP here is likely SECONDARY to SAH (Cushing response), not primary.

female-45-sah.oldcartsNarrative: "This is the worst headache of my life. It started suddenly about 2 hours ago - I was just sitting at my desk and suddenly felt like something exploded in my head. The pain was immediately at a 10 out of 10. I've never felt anything like this. Yes, I do get migraines, but this is completely different - my migraines come on gradually and build up over hours. This hit me like a thunderclap. My neck feels stiff and it hurts to move it. I feel nauseous. The light hurts my eyes. I took my migraine medication but it didn't help at all. No I didn't hit my head. I'm terrified - something feels really wrong."

historyQuestions.text: Describe exactly how the headache started.
historyQuestions.answer: I was just sitting at my desk working and suddenly - like a switch was flipped - I had the most intense headache of my life. It was immediately at maximum intensity. Like someone hit me in the head with a bat, or something exploded inside.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 🚨 THUNDERCLAP HEADACHE = SAH until proven otherwise. Sudden onset to maximal intensity within seconds is the defining feature.

historyQuestions.text: How does this compare to other headaches you've had?
historyQuestions.answer: This is completely different. I get migraines but they build up gradually. This was instant and way worse than any migraine. This is the worst headache of my entire life, no question.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 'WORST HEADACHE OF LIFE' is the classic description of SAH. Patient explicitly distinguishes from typical migraines = red flag.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your neck.
historyQuestions.answer: My neck is really stiff. It hurts to bend my chin to my chest. It's felt that way since the headache started.
historyQuestions.reasoning: NECK STIFFNESS (meningismus) occurs when blood irritates the meninges. Key physical finding in SAH.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any weakness, numbness, vision changes, or confusion?
historyQuestions.answer: No weakness or numbness. My vision is okay except that light hurts. I feel scared but I'm thinking clearly.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Absence of focal deficits doesn't rule out SAH. Focal findings would suggest parenchymal hemorrhage or aneurysm compressing brain.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned taking migraine medication - what happened?
historyQuestions.answer: I took my sumatriptan as soon as the headache started, like I always do for migraines. It didn't help at all. That's never happened before - my sumatriptan usually works.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Failure of usual migraine treatment in someone with different headache pattern - another red flag.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: I have high blood pressure but I take medication for it. I also get migraines. I smoke about half a pack a day. My mom had a brain aneurysm.
historyQuestions.reasoning: RISK FACTORS FOR SAH: HTN, smoking, and especially family history of aneurysm. First-degree relative with aneurysm significantly increases risk.

physicalExam.finding: BP 178/102 (elevated), HR 96, RR 18, Temp 99.1°F (low-grade fever), SpO2 97%.
physicalExam.reasoning: ELEVATED BP can be secondary to SAH (Cushing response to increased ICP). Low-grade fever can occur with blood in CSF.

physicalExam.finding: 45-year-old woman appearing very uncomfortable, holding her head, lying still in darkened room. Clearly in significant distress.
physicalExam.reasoning: Severe distress with preference for darkness. Patient's own sense that 'something is really wrong' should be taken seriously.

physicalExam.finding: Alert and oriented. CN II-XII intact. Pupils equal and reactive. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Reflexes symmetric.
physicalExam.reasoning: Non-focal neuro exam does NOT rule out SAH. Many SAH patients have normal exam except for meningismus.

physicalExam.finding: NUCHAL RIGIDITY present - resistance to passive neck flexion. Positive Kernig's sign (pain with knee extension when hip flexed). Positive Brudzinski's sign (involuntary hip flexion when neck flexed).
physicalExam.reasoning: 🚨 MENINGISMUS confirms meningeal irritation. In context of thunderclap headache = SAH until proven otherwise.

physicalExam.finding: Disc margins sharp. Possible subtle subhyaloid hemorrhages near disc (Terson syndrome). No papilledema.
physicalExam.reasoning: SUBHYALOID HEMORRHAGE (Terson syndrome) occurs in 10-25% of SAH from sudden ICP increase rupturing retinal vessels. Specific finding.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Baseline labs. Mild leukocytosis can occur with SAH.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: Important to know coagulation status if procedural interventions needed.

labs.name: Type and Screen
labs.reasoning: Blood availability important if intervention needed.

imaging.name: CT Head without contrast
imaging.result: SUBTLE HYPERDENSITY in basal cisterns and sylvian fissures bilaterally, consistent with acute subarachnoid hemorrhage.
imaging.reasoning: CT is first-line imaging for SAH. Sensitive for acute SAH (~95% in first 6 hours, but sensitivity decreases over time).

imaging.name: CT Angiogram (CTA)
imaging.result: 6mm saccular aneurysm at anterior communicating artery (ACom). No evidence of vasospasm.
imaging.reasoning: Once SAH confirmed, CTA identifies aneurysm location to guide treatment. ACom is most common location.

imaging.name: Lumbar Puncture
imaging.result: Would be required if CT were negative. CSF would show elevated RBCs and xanthochromia.
imaging.reasoning: LP required if CT negative but clinical suspicion high. Xanthochromia (yellow CSF from bilirubin) confirms SAH.

treatments.name: Nimodipine
treatments.result: Nimodipine 60mg PO q4h started for vasospasm prophylaxis.
treatments.reasoning: NIMODIPINE reduces vasospasm and improves outcomes in SAH. Standard of care.

treatments.name: Blood pressure control
treatments.result: Nicardipine drip started. Goal SBP <160 until aneurysm secured.
treatments.reasoning: BP control reduces rebleed risk. Balance needed - don't drop too low (cerebral perfusion).

treatments.name: Analgesia
treatments.result: Fentanyl IV for pain. Avoiding medications that mask neuro exam changes.
treatments.reasoning: Pain control important but use short-acting agents to allow neuro monitoring.

treatments.name: Seizure prophylaxis
treatments.result: Levetiracetam started for seizure prophylaxis.
treatments.reasoning: Seizure prophylaxis often given acutely in SAH given seizure risk.

consults.name: Neurosurgery STAT
consults.result: Neurosurgery consulted. Patient being evaluated for surgical clipping vs endovascular coiling of aneurysm.
consults.reasoning: EMERGENT neurosurgery consultation for aneurysm treatment planning. Goal is to secure aneurysm within 24 hours.

disposition.name: Admit to Neurosurgical ICU
disposition.result: Admitted to neuro ICU for close monitoring. Coiling planned for tomorrow AM.
disposition.reasoning: ICU monitoring required. Rebleed risk highest in first 24 hours. Close neuro checks for vasospasm (days 4-14).

actualDiagnosis.name: Subarachnoid Hemorrhage from Ruptured Anterior Communicating Artery Aneurysm
keyFindings: ⚠️ TRAP CASE: Patient has migraine history but THIS headache is different
THUNDERCLAP HEADACHE - sudden onset to maximal intensity
'WORST HEADACHE OF LIFE' - classic SAH description
MENINGISMUS - nuchal rigidity, positive Kernig's and Brudzinski's
Elevated BP (Cushing response)
Risk factors: HTN, smoking, family history of aneurysm
CT: blood in basal cisterns; CTA: ACom aneurysm
teachingPoints: ⚠️ MIGRAINE HISTORY DOESN'T PROTECT FROM SAH: Ask how this headache differs from typical migraines. 'Worst headache of life' is always a red flag.
THUNDERCLAP ONSET IS THE KEY: SAH headache reaches maximal intensity within SECONDS. Migraines build over minutes to hours.
CT SENSITIVITY DECREASES OVER TIME: ~95% sensitive at 6 hours, ~80% at 24 hours, <50% at 1 week. LP REQUIRED if CT negative but suspicion high.
XANTHOCHROMIA IS DIAGNOSTIC: Yellow color in CSF from bilirubin (RBC breakdown) distinguishes SAH from traumatic tap. Present 6+ hours after bleed.
REBLEED IS THE ENEMY: 4% rebleed rate in first 24 hours, 50% mortality with rebleed. Early aneurysm securing is critical.
NIMODIPINE IS STANDARD: Calcium channel blocker reduces vasospasm (days 4-14) and improves outcomes. Only proven medical therapy.
'WORST HEADACHE OF LIFE' = SAH UNTIL PROVEN OTHERWISE: This is a medical emergency. Missed SAH is a common cause of malpractice claims.

guidelineReferences.name: AHA/ASA Guidelines for SAH Management 2023
keyPoints: Non-contrast CT head is initial test (sensitivity 95% at 6h, decreases over time)
LP required if CT negative but clinical suspicion high
Early aneurysm securing (<24h) reduces rebleeding risk
Nimodipine 60mg q4h for 21 days to reduce vasospasm
Target euvolemia; avoid hypovolemia and hypervolemia

guidelineReferences.name: Neurocritical Care Society Guidelines for SAH 2011
guidelineReferences.citation: Diringer MN et al. Neurocrit Care. 2011;15(2):211-240
keyPoints: Hunt and Hess or WFNS grade for severity classification
Monitor for delayed cerebral ischemia (days 4-14)
Transcranial Doppler for vasospasm screening
Avoid hypotension (SBP <90) and extreme hypertension
External ventricular drain for hydrocephalus management

closureScript.diagnosis: I have very important news. You've had bleeding around your brain - we call this a subarachnoid hemorrhage. We found it on your CT scan. You also have an aneurysm - a weak spot in one of the blood vessels in your brain - and that's what ruptured and caused the bleeding. I know you've had migraines before, but your instinct that this was different was absolutely right.
closureScript.plan: We're admitting you to our intensive care unit right now. The neurosurgery team is coming to see you. The priority is to treat that aneurysm to prevent another bleed. This will probably be done tomorrow through a minimally invasive procedure where they put a catheter into the aneurysm and fill it with coils to seal it off. In the meantime, we're giving you medications to protect your brain and control your blood pressure.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: While you're here, we'll be checking on you very frequently - every hour - to monitor for complications. Let us know immediately if your headache gets suddenly worse, if you develop weakness, vision changes, or trouble speaking, or if you feel confused.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: I know this is terrifying news. But you did exactly the right thing coming in immediately and insisting this wasn't your normal migraine. Catching this when we did gives us the best chance to prevent another bleed. The neurosurgery team here is excellent, and we're going to take very close care of you.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had fever and neck stiffness?
whatIfScenarios.description: Same severe headache but with 102°F fever and photophobia

chiefComplaint.original: This headache is different - it's the worst headache I've ever had
chiefComplaint.modified: I have the worst headache ever with fever and the light hurts my eyes

feedback.correct: Fever + severe headache + photophobia = meningitis until proven otherwise. Immediate LP and empiric antibiotics required.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of concerning features. What's the management priority?
feedback.incorrect: This triad (fever, headache, photophobia/neck stiffness) mandates immediate LP. Don't delay antibiotics for imaging unless focal neuro deficit or papilledema.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: SAH = thunderclap + neck stiffness but usually afebrile. Meningitis = fever + headache + photophobia/meningismus. Both need LP but meningitis requires immediate antibiotics - every hour of delay increases mortality.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if fundoscopy showed papilledema?
whatIfScenarios.description: Gradual headache worsening over weeks with blurred vision and papilledema

chiefComplaint.original: This headache is different - it's the worst headache I've ever had
chiefComplaint.modified: My headaches have been getting worse over the past month and now my vision is blurry

feedback.correct: Papilledema = elevated ICP. Subacute course suggests mass lesion, IIH, or venous thrombosis. Urgent MRI with MRV needed.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of papilledema significance. What does the chronic course suggest?
feedback.incorrect: Papilledema indicates elevated ICP - this is NEVER benign. Subacute headache + papilledema = mass lesion until proven otherwise. Must rule out tumor, IIH, venous thrombosis.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Thunderclap = sudden vascular event (SAH, dissection). Subacute + papilledema = mass effect/ICP issue. Never do LP with papilledema until imaging rules out mass - risk of herniation.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the headache was worse when upright?
whatIfScenarios.description: Severe headache that improves dramatically when lying flat

chiefComplaint.original: This headache is different - it's the worst headache I've ever had
chiefComplaint.modified: I have a terrible headache when I sit or stand but it gets better when I lie down

feedback.correct: Postural headache (better lying down) is classic for low CSF pressure/leak. Ask about recent LP. MRI may show pachymeningeal enhancement.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of postural component. What causes headache that improves with lying flat?
feedback.incorrect: Orthostatic headache = low CSF pressure. Usually post-LP but can be spontaneous leak. MRI brain and spine can help localize. Treatment: caffeine, blood patch.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: SAH/meningitis = headache regardless of position. Low-pressure headache = dramatically better when supine. This distinction is critical for diagnosis and guides completely different management.

guidelineReferences.name: IDSA Group A Streptococcal Pharyngitis Guidelines 2012
guidelineReferences.citation: Shulman ST, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2012;55(10):e86-e102
keyPoints: Use Centor/McIsaac criteria for testing decisions
Rapid antigen test first; backup throat culture if negative in children
Penicillin or amoxicillin first-line for confirmed GAS
10 days of treatment to prevent rheumatic fever
Avoid antibiotics for viral pharyngitis
Carriers do not require treatment

teen-16-strep.name: J. Rodriguez
teen-16-strep.chiefComplaint: Really bad sore throat for 2 days

missedConsequences.Strep Pharyngitis: If untreated, risk of rheumatic fever (though rare in developed countries), post-strep glomerulonephritis, peritonsillar abscess.
missedConsequences.Peritonsillar Abscess: If PTA were present and missed, could progress to airway compromise, retropharyngeal spread, sepsis.
missedConsequences.Infectious Mononucleosis: If mono and given amoxicillin, patient develops diffuse rash.

commonPitfalls: Using Centor criteria incorrectly - they guide testing, not empiric treatment
Prescribing antibiotics without testing - rapid strep or culture should guide therapy
Missing the tonsillar exudates on exam - must look carefully with good lighting
Forgetting to check for splenomegaly in suspected mono - contact sports restriction needed
Not asking about sick contacts - strep spreads in households and schools

mcqs.question: This patient has fever, tonsillar exudates, tender anterior cervical lymph nodes, and NO cough. His Centor score is 4. What is the appropriate next step?

options.text: Treat empirically with antibiotics - Centor 4 is high enough
options.explanation: Even with high Centor, guidelines recommend testing before treatment. Centor guides who to test, not who to treat empirically.

options.text: Rapid strep test - treat if positive
options.explanation: Centor 4 justifies testing. Rapid strep test has high specificity - treat if positive. If negative, backup culture or clinical judgment.

options.text: No testing needed - treat all sore throats with antibiotics
options.explanation: This leads to massive antibiotic overuse. Most sore throats are viral.

options.text: CT scan to rule out abscess
options.explanation: No features suggesting abscess (trismus, uvular deviation, muffled voice).

mcqs.question: Rapid strep is positive. The patient is allergic to penicillin (hives). What antibiotic should you prescribe?

options.text: Amoxicillin - it's different from penicillin
options.explanation: Amoxicillin is a penicillin - cross-reactivity with true penicillin allergy.

options.text: Azithromycin or cephalosporin
options.explanation: For penicillin allergy with hives (Type I): azithromycin is preferred. Cephalosporins (cephalexin) can be used cautiously if reaction was minor/not Type I.

options.text: No antibiotic - strep doesn't need treatment
options.explanation: Strep should be treated to prevent complications and reduce transmission.

options.text: Fluoroquinolone
options.explanation: Not recommended in children/adolescents due to tendon/cartilage risks.

allDiagnoses.name: Streptococcal Pharyngitis (Strep Throat)
allDiagnoses.rationale: ⭐ ACTUAL DIAGNOSIS. Centor 4: fever, exudates, tender anterior nodes, no cough. High probability of strep.

allDiagnoses.name: Viral Pharyngitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Most common cause of sore throat overall, but this presentation has features suggesting bacterial.

allDiagnoses.name: Infectious Mononucleosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Consider in adolescents with pharyngitis. Would expect more fatigue, posterior nodes, possible splenomegaly.

allDiagnoses.name: Peritonsillar Abscess
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect unilateral swelling, trismus, uvular deviation, 'hot potato' voice. Absent here.

allDiagnoses.name: Epiglottitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Rare in vaccinated populations. Would expect drooling, stridor, tripod positioning. Absent here.

allDiagnoses.name: Gonococcal Pharyngitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Consider in sexually active patients. Usually less symptomatic than strep.

teen-16-strep.oldcartsNarrative: "My throat has been really sore for 2 days. It hurts to swallow - even drinking water hurts. I've had a fever, my mom checked and it was 102 yesterday. I have some swollen glands in my neck that are tender when I push on them. I feel tired but otherwise okay. My friend at school had strep last week. No, I'm not sexually active. I'm allergic to penicillin - I got hives when I took it as a kid."

historyQuestions.text: When did the sore throat start?
historyQuestions.answer: About 2 days ago. It came on pretty fast - I was fine in the morning and by that evening my throat was killing me.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rapid onset over 1-2 days typical of bacterial pharyngitis.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had a fever?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, my mom checked yesterday and it was 102. I've been feeling hot and cold.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fever is one of the Centor criteria. Present here = +1 point.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have a cough, runny nose, or congestion?
historyQuestions.answer: No, none of that. It's just my throat.
historyQuestions.reasoning: ABSENCE OF COUGH is Centor criterion. No cough = +1 point. Cough suggests viral etiology.

historyQuestions.text: Has anyone around you been sick?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, my friend at school was diagnosed with strep last week. A few other kids have been out sick too.
historyQuestions.reasoning: SICK CONTACTS with confirmed strep significantly increases probability.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm allergic to penicillin. When I was little, I took it for an ear infection and got hives all over.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Penicillin allergy affects treatment choice. Hives (Type I) = avoid penicillins.

historyQuestions.text: Have you been extremely tired, or noticed any belly pain?
historyQuestions.answer: I'm a little tired but nothing extreme. No belly pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Screening for mono symptoms. Profound fatigue and splenomegaly suggest mono.

historyQuestions.text: Can you swallow your saliva? Any trouble breathing?
historyQuestions.answer: I can swallow but it really hurts. No trouble breathing at all.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Drooling/inability to swallow or breathing difficulty are red flags for airway emergency

historyQuestions.text: Has your voice changed?
historyQuestions.answer: Maybe a little hoarse but nothing major. Not muffled or anything.
historyQuestions.reasoning: 'Hot potato' muffled voice suggests peritonsillar abscess

historyQuestions.text: Can you open your mouth all the way?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, I can open my mouth fine. No problem.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Trismus (difficulty opening mouth) is key sign of peritonsillar abscess

historyQuestions.text: Any other medical problems?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I'm healthy. I play baseball and do sports physicals every year.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Confirms otherwise healthy patient; no immunocompromise

historyQuestions.text: Are you taking any medications?
historyQuestions.answer: Nothing. Just some Tylenol for the fever and pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medication review; no immunosuppressants

historyQuestions.text: Are your vaccinations up to date?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, I think so. My mom makes sure I get everything for school.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Vaccination status - Hib vaccine has made epiglottitis rare

historyQuestions.text: Have you had strep throat before?
historyQuestions.answer: Once or twice when I was younger. Got antibiotics and it went away.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior strep history; recurrent strep may need different management approach

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

social_exposure_setting.answer: High school. It's been going around. Three kids on my baseball team have had strep this month.
social_exposure_setting.relevance: School outbreaks of strep are common. Multiple confirmed strep cases in close contacts dramatically increases probability.

recreation_sports_activities.answer: I play baseball. Practice every day, games on weekends. We share water bottles sometimes.
recreation_sports_activities.relevance: Sports teams are high-transmission settings; shared water bottles facilitate bacterial spread.

housing_household_size.answer: Live with mom, dad, and my younger sister. My sister was just sick last week.
housing_household_size.relevance: Household exposure increases transmission risk. Sister's recent illness may be index case.

social_personal_items.answer: I might have shared a drink with my friend who had strep. Didn't think about it.
social_personal_items.relevance: Direct contact with strep-positive individual's items is high-risk exposure.

occupation_school_attendance.answer: I've been going to school even though I felt bad. Didn't want to miss practice.
occupation_school_attendance.relevance: Presenteeism perpetuates outbreaks. Important to counsel about staying home when symptomatic.

social_sleep_patterns.answer: Not great. Staying up late with homework and games. Maybe 6 hours on school nights.
social_sleep_patterns.relevance: Sleep deprivation impairs immune function and may increase susceptibility to infection.

diet_nutrition.answer: I eat okay. School lunch, dinner at home. Lots of snacks. Energy drinks sometimes.
diet_nutrition.relevance: Typical teenage diet; not a major factor in pharyngitis.

social_immunization_status.answer: I think I'm up to date. My mom handles all that.
social_immunization_status.relevance: Vaccination status important for ruling out other causes but no vaccine for strep pharyngitis.

physicalExam.reasoning: Fever present = Centor +1.

physicalExam.finding: 16-year-old male appearing mildly ill but non-toxic. Sitting comfortably, no distress, no drooling.
physicalExam.reasoning: Non-toxic appearance, no drooling = argues against epiglottitis and severe abscess.

physicalExam.finding: Erythematous pharynx. TONSILLAR EXUDATES present bilaterally (white patches on both tonsils). Tonsils 3+ enlarged. Uvula midline. No trismus. Palate symmetric.
physicalExam.reasoning: TONSILLAR EXUDATES = Centor +1. Symmetric exam argues against peritonsillar abscess.

physicalExam.finding: TENDER ANTERIOR CERVICAL LYMPHADENOPATHY bilaterally, 1-2cm nodes. Posterior cervical nodes not enlarged.
physicalExam.reasoning: TENDER ANTERIOR NODES = Centor +1. Posterior nodes would suggest mono.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-tender. No hepatosplenomegaly.
physicalExam.reasoning: No splenomegaly argues against mono. Important because splenomegaly = contact sports restriction.

labs.name: Rapid Strep Antigen Test
labs.reasoning: Rapid strep is FIRST-LINE diagnostic test. Sensitivity 70-90%, specificity >95%. POSITIVE test = treat. No need for culture if positive.

labs.name: Throat Culture
labs.result: Sent as backup (not needed if rapid positive). Would confirm GAS if rapid negative.
labs.reasoning: Gold standard but takes 24-48 hours. SEND IF: rapid strep negative but clinical suspicion high (Centor 3-4). Not needed if rapid positive.

labs.name: Monospot (Heterophile Antibody)
labs.result: Not indicated - rapid strep already positive and no mono features.
labs.reasoning: Would consider if: posterior cervical adenopathy, splenomegaly, prolonged fatigue, petechiae on palate. Not needed here.

labs.name: CBC (if mono suspected)
labs.result: Not indicated for routine strep pharyngitis.
labs.reasoning: Would show atypical lymphocytes if mono. Not needed for uncomplicated strep.

imaging.name: No imaging indicated
imaging.result: Imaging not needed for uncomplicated pharyngitis.
imaging.reasoning: No imaging needed for strep throat. Would only image if concern for deep space neck infection (peritonsillar abscess, parapharyngeal abscess).

imaging.name: CT Neck with contrast (if abscess suspected)
imaging.result: NOT indicated - symmetric exam, no trismus, midline uvula.
imaging.reasoning: Would order if concern for peritonsillar or retropharyngeal abscess. Signs: trismus, uvular deviation, unilateral bulging, 'hot potato' voice, drooling.

treatments.name: Azithromycin 500mg day 1, then 250mg days 2-5
treatments.result: Azithromycin Z-pack prescribed for 5-day course.
treatments.reasoning: FIRST-LINE for penicillin allergy with Type I reaction (hives, anaphylaxis). Covers GAS. Complete full course.

treatments.name: Penicillin VK 500mg BID x 10 days (if no allergy)
treatments.result: WOULD be first choice if no PCN allergy - not used here due to allergy.
treatments.reasoning: Penicillin is first-line for strep - narrow spectrum, cheap, no resistance. Also amoxicillin 1g daily x 10 days.

treatments.name: Cephalexin 500mg BID x 10 days (if non-Type I PCN allergy)
treatments.result: Could consider if PCN allergy was non-severe (rash only, not hives/anaphylaxis).
treatments.reasoning: First-gen cephalosporins have ~1% cross-reactivity with PCN. Safe in non-Type I (delayed) reactions. Avoid in anaphylaxis history.

treatments.name: Ibuprofen 400mg PO q6h PRN
treatments.result: Ibuprofen 400mg q6h as needed for pain and fever.
treatments.reasoning: NSAIDs superior to acetaminophen for pharyngitis pain. Also reduces fever. Can alternate with Tylenol.

treatments.name: Acetaminophen 650mg q4-6h PRN
treatments.result: Acetaminophen as additional option for fever/pain.
treatments.reasoning: Alternative analgesic/antipyretic. Can alternate with ibuprofen.

treatments.name: Encourage Oral Fluids
treatments.result: Counseled on hydration. Cold fluids, popsicles, ice chips may soothe throat.
treatments.reasoning: Supportive care. Cold fluids provide some anesthetic effect on throat.

treatments.name: Salt Water Gargles
treatments.result: Warm salt water gargles (1/2 tsp salt in 8oz water) recommended.
treatments.reasoning: Traditional remedy with mild benefit for sore throat. Low cost, no risk.

treatments.name: Throat Lozenges (menthol/benzocaine)
treatments.result: Throat lozenges PRN for symptomatic relief.
treatments.reasoning: Topical anesthetics provide temporary pain relief. Menthol provides cooling sensation.

treatments.name: Dexamethasone 10mg x 1 (controversial)
treatments.result: Consider single dose of dexamethasone for severe pain.
treatments.reasoning: Some evidence for faster pain relief. Not routinely recommended but may consider for very severe odynophagia.

consults.name: ENT Referral
consults.result: Not indicated for uncomplicated strep pharyngitis.
consults.reasoning: Would refer for: recurrent strep (≥7/year, ≥5/year x 2 years, or ≥3/year x 3 years) for tonsillectomy consideration. Also for peritonsillar abscess.

disposition.name: Discharge Home
disposition.result: Discharged with azithromycin, symptomatic treatment, and return precautions.
disposition.reasoning: Outpatient management for uncomplicated strep pharyngitis.

disposition.name: Return to School Guidance
disposition.result: May return to school 24 hours after starting antibiotics AND fever-free.
disposition.reasoning: 24-hour rule reduces transmission. Patient is no longer contagious after 24h of antibiotics.

disposition.name: Household Contact Guidance
disposition.result: Advised that household members with symptoms should be tested. Prophylaxis not routinely indicated.
disposition.reasoning: Test symptomatic contacts. Prophylaxis only in outbreaks or if household member has history of rheumatic fever.

disposition.name: Return Precautions
disposition.result: Return if: worsening pain despite antibiotics, difficulty breathing or swallowing, inability to open mouth (trismus), drooling, rash, not improving in 48-72 hours.
disposition.reasoning: Red flags for peritonsillar abscess or treatment failure. Rash could indicate scarlet fever or drug reaction.

disposition.name: Follow-up
disposition.result: No routine follow-up needed if symptoms resolve. Test of cure not indicated.
disposition.reasoning: Post-treatment throat culture not routinely recommended unless persistent/recurrent symptoms.

actualDiagnosis.name: Streptococcal Pharyngitis (Strep Throat)
keyFindings: Centor score 4: Fever + Exudates + Anterior nodes + No cough
Rapid onset sore throat with odynophagia
Sick contacts with confirmed strep
Bilateral tonsillar exudates on exam
Tender anterior cervical lymphadenopathy
POSITIVE rapid strep test
teachingPoints: CENTOR CRITERIA guide testing, not empiric treatment: Fever (+1), Tonsillar exudates (+1), Tender anterior cervical nodes (+1), Absence of cough (+1). Score 3-4 = test, treat if positive.
MOST SORE THROATS ARE VIRAL: Only ~10% of adult sore throats are strep. Don't give antibiotics without testing.
RAPID STREP IS HIGHLY SPECIFIC: Positive result = treat. Negative result in high-risk patient = consider backup culture.
PENICILLIN IS FIRST-LINE: For non-allergic patients, penicillin V or amoxicillin. For PCN allergy: azithromycin or cephalosporin (if not Type I).
COMPLICATIONS ARE RARE BUT REAL: Rheumatic fever (rare in US), glomerulonephritis, peritonsillar abscess. Treatment reduces suppurative complications.
ABSENCE OF COUGH IS KEY: Cough, rhinorrhea, conjunctivitis suggest viral etiology. Their absence supports bacterial.
ANTERIOR VS POSTERIOR NODES: Anterior = more strep. Posterior = think mononucleosis.

guidelineReferences.name: IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline for GAS Pharyngitis 2012
guidelineReferences.citation: Shulman ST et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2012;55(10):e86-e102
keyPoints: Test patients with clinical features of GAS pharyngitis (Centor 3-4)
Penicillin or amoxicillin is first-line treatment
10-day course for penicillin; 5-day Z-pack for PCN allergy
Backup culture if negative rapid test in children
Treatment prevents rheumatic fever if started within 9 days

guidelineReferences.name: AHA Guideline on Prevention of Rheumatic Fever 2009
keyPoints: Primary prevention: Treat GAS pharyngitis with appropriate antibiotics
Penicillin V 500mg BID x 10 days or Amoxicillin 50mg/kg x 10 days
First-generation cephalosporins for non-anaphylactic PCN allergy
Macrolides for true PCN allergy
Test of cure not routinely indicated

centor.name: Centor Criteria (Modified/McIsaac)
centor.description: Estimates probability of GAS pharyngitis to guide testing and treatment

thisPatient.nextStep: Test with rapid strep; consider empiric treatment if test unavailable
thisPatient.clinicalAction: Rapid strep test is positive - treat with antibiotics.

intermediate.action: Test with rapid strep; treat if positive

high.action: Test with rapid strep; consider empiric treatment

closureScript.diagnosis: You have strep throat. The rapid test we did came back positive for streptococcal bacteria. This explains your sore throat, fever, and swollen glands.
closureScript.plan: Since you're allergic to penicillin, I'm prescribing azithromycin - take it for 5 days as directed. You should start feeling better within 24-48 hours. You can take ibuprofen for the pain and fever. Drink plenty of fluids - cold drinks and popsicles may feel good.
closureScript.warningsToDiscuss: Come back if the pain gets worse instead of better, if you develop difficulty breathing or swallowing, if you start drooling or can't open your mouth, or if you develop a rash. You can go back to school 24 hours after starting the antibiotic as long as your fever is gone.
closureScript.addressingConcerns: Strep is very common and very treatable. The antibiotic will clear the infection and also prevent you from spreading it to others. Your friend at school probably gave it to you, so let anyone at home know they should watch for symptoms too.

patientBackground.recentEvents: Patient was in usual state of health until symptoms began. No major recent life changes.
patientBackground.recentIllnesses: No significant recent illnesses other than this presentation.
patientBackground.contextualFactors: Otherwise healthy and active. This illness is unexpected and concerning.
patientBackground.supportSystem: Young adult with supportive family and friends. Active social life.
patientBackground.healthBeliefs: Generally trusts medical advice. Wants to understand the reasoning behind recommendations.
patientBackground.previousERVisits: Uses healthcare appropriately. No excessive ER utilization.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient couldn't open their mouth and had a 'hot potato' voice?
whatIfScenarios.description: Severe sore throat with trismus, drooling, and muffled voice

chiefComplaint.original: My throat is really sore and I have a fever
chiefComplaint.modified: My throat is killing me, I can barely open my mouth, and I'm drooling because I can't swallow

feedback.correct: Trismus + muffled voice + drooling = peritonsillar abscess. Look for uvula deviation. ENT for I&D (needle aspiration or incision). IV antibiotics. May need CT if diagnosis uncertain. Watch airway!
feedback.partial: Good recognition of PTA. What's the treatment?
feedback.incorrect: Peritonsillar abscess = strep that's spread beyond the tonsil. Classic triad: trismus, 'hot potato' voice, drooling. Uvula deviates AWAY from abscess. Treatment: needle aspiration or I&D + IV antibiotics. ENT consult. Watch for airway compromise.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Simple strep = symmetric exam, can open mouth, no drooling. PTA = unilateral bulge, trismus, deviation, muffled voice. PTA is a complication of strep that requires procedural intervention.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had fatigue and posterior lymphadenopathy?
whatIfScenarios.description: Sore throat with extreme fatigue, splenomegaly, and posterior cervical nodes

chiefComplaint.original: My throat is really sore and I have a fever
chiefComplaint.modified: My throat is sore but I'm also extremely tired - I've been sleeping 14 hours a day. My glands are swollen everywhere.

feedback.correct: Fatigue + posterior lymphadenopathy + pharyngitis = think MONO. Check heterophile (Monospot) and CBC (atypical lymphocytes). Counsel to avoid contact sports for 4-6 weeks (splenic rupture risk). NO amoxicillin (causes rash).
feedback.partial: Good recognition of mono. What are the key counseling points?
feedback.incorrect: Mononucleosis clues: POSTERIOR cervical nodes (strep is anterior), extreme fatigue, splenomegaly, possible rash. Confirm with Monospot or EBV titers. Key counseling: NO contact sports (splenic rupture), avoid amoxicillin (causes rash in mono). Supportive care only.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Strep = anterior cervical nodes, less fatigue, responds to antibiotics. Mono = posterior nodes, profound fatigue, splenomegaly, supportive care only. Giving amoxicillin to mono causes diffuse rash.

whatIfScenarios.title: What if the patient had stridor and tripod positioning?
whatIfScenarios.description: Adult with severe sore throat, stridor, drooling, and sitting forward

chiefComplaint.original: My throat is really sore and I have a fever
chiefComplaint.modified: I can't swallow at all, I'm having trouble breathing, there's a high-pitched sound when I breathe

feedback.correct: EPIGLOTTITIS = AIRWAY EMERGENCY. Don't examine throat (can precipitate complete obstruction). Keep patient calm, call anesthesia/ENT, prepare for emergency airway. Lateral neck X-ray shows 'thumb sign' if imaging done. IV antibiotics after airway secured.
feedback.partial: Good recognition of epiglottitis. What should you NOT do?
feedback.incorrect: Epiglottitis in adults: stridor, tripod position, drooling, severe odynophagia out of proportion to pharyngeal exam. AIRWAY EMERGENCY. Do NOT examine throat aggressively (can precipitate obstruction). Call anesthesia/ENT, prepare emergency airway. 'Thumb sign' on lateral neck X-ray.

whatIfScenarios.comparisonNotes: Pharyngitis/PTA = visible abnormality in throat, no stridor. Epiglottitis = minimal pharyngeal findings, STRIDOR (supraglottic), airway emergency. Stridor changes everything - this is above the vocal cords.
